abstract: Lipopeptides represent a versatile class of amphiphilic molecules characterized by a peptide headgroup coupled to one or more lipid chains. This review examines the biophysical properties and diverse biomedical applications of both naturally occurring and synthetic lipopeptides. Two primary categories are analyzed: bacterially-derived cyclic lipopeptides, such as daptomycin and surfactin, which function as potent antimicrobial and antifungal agents; and linear lipopeptides, including Toll-like receptor agonists used in immunotherapy and bio-inspired sequences like Matrixyl utilized in cosmeceuticals. A central focus is placed on the self-assembly behavior of these molecules; depending on molecular architecture and environmental factors like pH and temperature, they spontaneously organize into various supramolecular nanostructures, ranging from spherical and wormlike micelles to extended bilayer nanotapes and fibrils. The article further explores current understanding regarding the interplay between these self-assembled morphologies and biological efficacy, highlighting how the high-density presentation of peptide motifs on nanostructured surfaces may influence their therapeutic performance and interaction with cell membranes.
